item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as it comes from here with a non-compliant license. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Can't they use AIAD to find out if it's a perfect sphere?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: understandable, have a broke day

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: telekill machine broke

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Use summa DAT telekill alloy so you can do the measurement boi

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's what they WANT you to think

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I propose that this SCP is vote locked if it reaches three hundred and fourteen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, if they already took samples, it can't be a perfect one anymore? There, question about whether it really is perfect or just memetic answered. Or I'm just making a fool of myself because I understood it the wrong way, could be either.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The Foundation going crazy over an anomaly even though that's what they always deal with seems far fetched to me. However, I do like to believe that it is a perfect sphere and that the knowledge of this is an info hazard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do u want to build a sphere?
It doesn't have to be a sphere…

Oh wait, it must be a sphere, a perfect sphere, a spherest sphere…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: An extreme example of wondering whether or not a perfect sphere is possible, I like it, however I prefer when things are at least a little more subtle. Good work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not upvoting, because it leaves me generally unimpressed, but knowing the contest constraints it was written within… damn! I'm no-voting as I would any other article, but with what the author had to work with, this is fantastic!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'd like to see a case study where someone who isn't originally skeptical attempts to prove that the object indeed is a 'perfect sphere'. If they are affected the same as the other researchers then the object's memetic properties can be proven and the object becomes that much more profound.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That was exactly what was running through my head after the very last line. Maybe we aren't level 4 personnel. What a twist. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea that there's no memetic effect, and it is just a mathematically perfect sphere, and the Foundation researchers are just so completely unwilling to admit that impossibility that they start making up wild excuses.

I mean, it's like the missing number of physics or something. It goes against everything they know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Holy shit if you measure the pixels in the picture in any direction along the sphere it's exactly the same

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is that a joke.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This actually reminds me of an idea I had for a joke SCP, based around the way that when you look into a shiny ceramic bowl it looks like there is a glass sphere inside it.

… has anyone else noticed that? or am I just crazy?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I thought that the memetic effect extended to the data itself, as in reading or hearing the information about the spherical properties of the sphere caused it to spread, so in your experiment even if they were given no other information about the sphere, they would probably still exhibit its effects. I could always add something to make it a bit clearer.

Although I kind of like the ambiguity. I like the idea of having some things that are really hard to actually determine due to the nature of the object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It bugs me that it's not clear wether this is a perfect sphere or if it just extends an effect that makes folks trying to check think that it is perfect. Since the effect attaches to the ball, and to being told that the ball is perfect, couldn't this be confirmed one way or the other with a double-blind set-up? Researcher A takes the measurements, falling under the effects. The data, and no additional information, is transfered remotely to Researcher B. B has no knowledge of 1137, has been specifically quarantined from anyone even tangentially involved with it, and has been told that he will be undergoing intelligence testing in the form of interpreting manufactured data. If B concludes that the "fictional" data describes an impossible, perfectly round object, then we've proven 1137's nature. If B also develops an obsession with his "fictional" data set, then we can conclude that the memetic effect is even more viral than currently thought. Either way, a worth-while and relatively easy experiment that I'm certain the Foundation would try.

Or ignore my OCD tinkering, it's a perfectly acceptable article as it stands. I'd say you've definitely succeeded at the challenge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It reminds me of when I try drawing. Each line must be perfect. Must. Make. A. SPHERE. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Could be that the article is actually a cover story for the real nature of the thing. #mnightshalamamalantwist

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, maybe it is. I never really thought of that, although if it is actually a mathematically perfect sphere then the people going crazy are actually just being driven mad by the revelation, which seems like a bit too much of an old-school Lovecraft cliche to me. Although maybe it is actually a perfect sphere and the researchers just don't want to admit it, or that the people actually went crazy without a memetic trigger. I feel like that idea has been done before on other articles though, but I may be wrong.

Hmmm… food for thought I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Great article; can I just say thank you for not making a l33t joke with the number.

Also, is it not possible that this just is, contrary to our logic, a mathematically perfect sphere? Why is that so hard an idea for these people to reconcile; way stranger things that contradict everything we think we know happen every day where they work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good work on one of the more difficult Challenges.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I remember this one, and I still like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like how you take the two cliches and instead of trying to work your way around them, you just embrace them wholeheartedly, trying to rely on competent execution rather than strength of concept. A good effort. Without the challenge, I'm not sure if I'd upvote this, but with the challenge, I certainly shall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a sphere. But it is not just a sphere. This is the spherest of all spheres. This sphere's effect is that it is so much of a sphere that it makes people go insane due to it's spheredom. This is the absolute apex of everything what are spheres…but I love it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My only cinch is all that anti-memetic stuff at the end of the document.
Otherwise, solid concept, and excellent execution.
Well done.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of the few possible reasons to redact the containment protocols. Knowledge of what the containment protocols actually entail may actually spread the meme itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not bad. It definitely helps that being a sphere is significant to this object, as opposed to "random effect slapped onto what is, incidentally, a sphere".

EDIT: I hate my phone. That is all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this, a well-done response to one of Bright's challenges. +1 for you, sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this is pretty good considering the evil cliched, but you shouldn't redact in the containment protocols.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any particular spots where you think the writing could be tidied up?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think it's a novel take on the idea. Tighten up the writing a bit and I'll upvote it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This will probably be downvoted just for being what it is, but I actually liked it, good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote. Good for what it is though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
New SCP, done in response to Bright's Challenge #2, to pick two random items on the cliche list and combine them.
I rolled:
6. Things what make you go nuts for no apparent reason.
13: Anything what is spherical.
So this is a sphere what makes you go crazy.
